cards.text: The bitwise AND operator is {{c1::&}} and it returns 1 only if {{c2::both bits}} are 1.
The bitwise OR operator is {{c1::|}} and it returns 1 if {{c2::either bit}} (or both) is 1.
The bitwise XOR operator is {{c1::^}} and it returns 1 only if {{c2::the bits are different}} (one is 0 and the other is 1).

cards.question: What is the result in binary?
cards.explanation:
Bitwise AND compares each bit:
0101
0110
----
0100
(1 only where both bits are 1)

cards.question: What is the result in binary?
cards.explanation:
Bitwise OR compares each bit:
0101
0110
----
0111
(1 if either bit is 1)

cards.text: The bitwise NOT (complement) operator is {{c1::~}} and it {{c2::flips all bits}} (0 becomes 1, 1 becomes 0).
The left shift operator {{c1::<<}} shifts bits to the left by n positions, and fills the vacated bits with {{c2::0s}}.

cards.question: What is the result of this left shift?
cards.explanation:
Left shift by 2 moves all bits 2 positions left:
0011 becomes 1100
This is equivalent to multiplying by 2^2 = 4 (3 * 4 = 12)

cards.text: The right shift operator {{c1::>>}} shifts bits to the right by n positions. For {{c2::unsigned}} numbers, it fills with 0s.

cards.question: What is the result in binary?
cards.explanation:
Bitwise XOR compares each bit:
0101
0110
----
0011
(1 only where bits are different)

cards.front: What mathematical operation does left shift by n positions perform?
cards.back:
Multiplication by 2^n
Example: x << 3 multiplies x by 2^3 = 8

cards.front: What mathematical operation does right shift by n positions perform on unsigned integers?
cards.back:
Division by 2^n (integer division, rounds down)
Example: x >> 2 divides x by 2^2 = 4

cards.concept: Difference between bitwise and logical operators
cards.explanation:
Bitwise operators (&, |, ^, ~) work on individual bits. Logical operators (&&, ||, !) work on boolean values and use short-circuit evaluation.
  • Bitwise: 0b0101 & 0b0110 = 0b0100
  • Logical: true && false = false
Don't confuse & with && or | with ||!

cards.question: What is the result for an 8-bit value?
cards.explanation:
Bitwise NOT flips all bits:
0000 1111 becomes 1111 0000

cards.text: The six bitwise operators are: {{c1::&}} (AND), {{c2::|}} (OR), {{c3::^}} (XOR), {{c4::~}} (NOT), {{c5::<<}} (left shift), and {{c6::>>}} (right shift).
The compound bitwise assignment operators are: {{c1::&=}}, {{c2::|=}}, {{c3::^=}}, {{c4::<<=}}, and {{c5::>>=}}.
